text: [{'text': "I'll systematically analyze the RTL/source files in the input scope for permission-related security vulnerabilities.", 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01PEBArzzmidc7J8WMdNBCjm', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.'}, 'name': 'list_dir', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': 'Now let me read the rest of the rsa_wrapper.sv to see the read side:', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01LhF1vaAoGAjQNBUvtvqaGj', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'limit': 200, 'offset': 300, 'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rsa/rsa_wrapper.sv'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': 'Now let me read the full `riscv_peripherals.sv` to understand how permission controls are wired:', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_012w7obaepMDck97TVkrqK2e', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'limit': 200, 'offset': 200, 'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/openpiton/riscv_peripherals.sv'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': 'Now let me look at the important `rng_wrapper.sv` read side more carefully, specifically addressing the debug mode bypass:', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01DDFwm65hLVDzDYDVFsqLjX', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv', 'query': 'debug_mode_i'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}, {'id': 'toolu_01XQgkyTPuF4bu9ocuTFFgzi', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rsa/rsa_wrapper.sv', 'query': 'debug_mode_i'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': "Now I'll look at the PKT wrapper read side more carefully, especially the unchecked `rdata = fuse_rdata_i` at line 96:", 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01C9o4tRcUWmAWQDKRWwmNFE', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'limit': 60, 'offset': 85, 'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': 'Now I have found a critical vulnerability. Let me also examine the RNG wrapper for the `rand_num_big` read path (lines 180-182) where the `debug_mode_i` bypass is absent:', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01RohFWSRgbEvqmhBtLy31bn', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'limit': 60, 'offset': 150, 'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}, {'id': 'toolu_01HHRCfJJpqi2VLeHKsK2vc6', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_cs.v'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

{"analysis_summary": "Analyzed RTL/source files in the HackAtDac21 expansion scope, focusing on fuse_mem, rng_wrapper, rsa_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, pkt, and the top-level riscv_peripherals. Three distinct permission/access-control vulnerabilities were identified:

1. **PKT Wrapper – Unguarded Default Fuse Read (Missing Lock Check):** In `pkt_wrapper.sv`, the combinational read logic sets `rdata = fuse_rdata_i` *unconditionally* as the default before entering the case statement, meaning any address not covered by explicit case arms (0–4) will still drive raw fuse memory data out to the caller with no `reglk_ctrl_i` gating whatsoever.

2. **RNG Wrapper – Debug Mode Unconditionally Bypasses Register Lock on Write-Side:** In `rng_wrapper.sv`, the polynomial write logic uses `debug_mode_i ? wdata : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? old_val : wdata)`. When `debug_mode_i=1`, the write always proceeds regardless of the lock bit `reglk_ctrl_i[5]`. This means the debug signal completely overrides the intended write-lock protection on the polynomial configuration registers.

3. **RNG Wrapper – Debug Mode Unconditionally Exposes Seed and Internal State on Read-Side:** Multiple RNG read-side registers return sensitive values (`seed`, `poly128`, `poly64`, `poly32`, `poly16`, `seed128_big_o`, `seed64_big_o`, etc.) whenever `debug_mode_i=1`, completely bypassing the lock bits (`reglk_ctrl_i[3]`, `[5]`, `[7]`). An attacker who can assert `debug_mode_i` can read all internal RNG state regardless of lock settings.

All three issues share a root cause: privileged/sensitive data paths lack proper hierarchical gating, allowing either an unguarded default assignment (PKT) or an overly broad debug-mode bypass (RNG) to leak or allow manipulation of security-critical values.", "findings": [{"finding_id": "FIND-001", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "PKT Wrapper exposes raw fuse memory data at unconstrained default addresses without any register lock check", "vulnerability_category": "Unauthorized Read / Missing Access Control", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 92, "line_end": 113, "module": "pkt_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "rdata / fuse_rdata_i"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 94, "line_end": 96, "module": "pkt_wrapper", "object": "rdata default assignment", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Inside the combinational read block, `rdata = fuse_rdata_i` is set unconditionally as the pre-case default when `en` is asserted. No register lock bit (reglk_ctrl_i) gates this default.", "supports_claim": "Any AXI read to an address whose [7:3] field does not match cases 0–4 will return raw fuse memory data without any permission check."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 107, "line_end": 107, "module": "pkt_wrapper", "object": "case 4: reglk_ctrl_i[6] gating", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "The explicit case 4 for fuse_rdata_i does apply reglk_ctrl_i[6]. However the default path (line 96) does not, so addresses outside 0–4 skip this protection.", "supports_claim": "The access control for fuse data is only applied in one out of two code paths that expose fuse_rdata_i."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 108, "line_end": 110, "module": "pkt_wrapper", "object": "default case with fuse_addr_o range check", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "The default case returns 0 only if fuse_addr_o <= 110, but the rdata was already set to fuse_rdata_i at line 96 before entering this case; addresses outside 0–4 that don't satisfy the default condition still carry the fuse value.", "supports_claim": "The default assignment is never overwritten for out-of-range addresses with high fuse_addr_o values."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/fuse_mem/fuse_mem.sv", "line_start": 1, "line_end": 90, "module": "fuse_mem", "object": "fuse_mem contents", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "fuse_mem stores extremely sensitive data: JTAG HMAC hash, HMAC keys (ikey/okey), AES keys (AES0/AES1/AES2), SHA256 keys, RNG polynomials, and access control tables.", "supports_claim": "The data exposed through the unguarded default path is cryptographically sensitive."}], "reasoning_summary": "In pkt_wrapper.sv, the read always_comb block first sets `rdata = fuse_rdata_i` as a fallthrough default whenever `en` is high. The subsequent case statement provides per-register protection (reglk_ctrl_i[4]/[5]/[6]) for addresses 0–4, but any AXI read with address[7:3] outside {0,1,2,3,4} falls through to the default case where rdata is still carrying the unprotected fuse_rdata_i value. This means the entire fuse memory (including HMAC keys, AES keys, and SHA256 keys) is readable at any address above 4 without lock-bit protection.", "security_impact": "An attacker with access to the PKT peripheral AXI interface can read any fuse memory contents (HMAC keys, AES keys, SHA256 keys, RNG polynomials) by issuing reads to unmapped addresses (e.g., address[7:3] >= 5), bypassing all register-lock protections.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The fuse address used to select which fuse entry is returned is fuse_addr_o (a user-controlled register). The exact reachability through the NoC depends on acc_ctrl_c gating in riscv_peripherals.sv, but within the PKT wrapper itself the default assignment is unconditional.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Guard the default `rdata = fuse_rdata_i` assignment with the appropriate reglk_ctrl_i lock bit (e.g., reglk_ctrl_i[6]).", "Alternatively, initialize rdata to 64'b0 and only set it to fuse_rdata_i inside a guarded case arm.", "Ensure the default branch of the case statement always zeroes rdata regardless of fuse_addr_o range."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-002", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "RNG Wrapper debug mode unconditionally bypasses register lock on polynomial write operations", "vulnerability_category": "Privilege Escalation / Register Lock Bypass via Debug Mode", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 120, "line_end": 143, "module": "rng_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "poly128[3:0], poly64[1:0], poly32_big, poly16_big"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 127, "line_end": 127, "module": "rng_wrapper", "object": "poly128[3] write mux", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`poly128[3] <= debug_mode_i ? wdata[31:0] : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? poly128[3] : wdata[31:0]);` — when debug_mode_i=1, the write proceeds unconditionally regardless of reglk_ctrl_i[5].", "supports_claim": "The debug mode signal acts as a complete bypass of the register write lock bit reglk_ctrl_i[5]."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 129, "line_end": 141, "module": "rng_wrapper", "object": "poly128[2:0], poly64[1:0], poly32_big, poly16_big write mux", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "All seven additional polynomial registers use the same `debug_mode_i ? wdata : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? old_val : wdata)` pattern, confirming the lock bypass is pervasive across all RNG polynomial writes.", "supports_claim": "Every polynomial configuration register is unprotected when debug_mode_i is asserted."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/openpiton/riscv_peripherals.sv", "line_start": 1104, "line_end": 1113, "module": "riscv_peripherals", "object": "rng_wrapper instantiation", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "The rng_wrapper receives `debug_mode_i` directly from the top-level port without any privilege-level gating; the debug mode is connected to a system-wide signal that could be asserted by an attacker who can influence the debug interface.", "supports_claim": "The debug_mode_i bypass is reachable from the system boundary."}], "reasoning_summary": "In rng_wrapper.sv, every polynomial register write uses a 2-level mux: `debug_mode_i ? wdata : (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? old_val : wdata)`. When debug_mode_i is asserted, the outer mux always selects wdata — meaning the register lock (reglk_ctrl_i[5]) is ignored entirely. An adversary who can assert debug_mode_i can modify RNG polynomials even after the lock bit has been set, potentially weakening or biasing the random number generator.", "security_impact": "If an attacker can assert debug_mode_i (e.g., via JTAG or a compromised debug interface), they can modify the LFSR polynomial registers (poly128, poly64, poly32, poly16) even when the firmware has locked them via reglk_ctrl_i[5]. This allows deliberate weakening of the random number generation, leading to predictable keys and nonces for cryptographic operations.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The exploitability depends on whether an attacker can assert debug_mode_i through an accessible interface. The scope does not include the JTAG authentication module source, so the strength of the JTAG lock (jtag_unlock) and its relationship to debug_mode_i cannot be fully confirmed from available files.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Remove the unconditional debug_mode_i bypass from polynomial write muxes; if debug writes are needed, gate them with an authenticated debug credential.", "Ensure debug_mode_i cannot override a locked register. The intended pattern should be: `reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? old_val : wdata` without any debug bypass.", "Audit how debug_mode_i is generated and whether it can be asserted without proper authentication."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-003", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "RNG Wrapper debug mode unconditionally exposes all internal seeds and state on read-side, bypassing register lock bits", "vulnerability_category": "Sensitive Data Exposure / Register Lock Bypass via Debug Mode", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 150, "line_end": 207, "module": "rng_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "seed[0:3], poly128_big, poly64_big, poly32_big, poly16_big, seed128_big_o, seed64_big_o, seed32_big_o, seed16_big_o, rand_seg_o, cs_state_o"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 156, "line_end": 162, "module": "rng_wrapper", "object": "seed[3:0] read mux (addresses 0-3)", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "`rdata = debug_mode_i ? seed[3] : (reglk_ctrl_i[3] ? 0 : seed[3])` — when debug_mode_i=1 the seed value is always returned regardless of lock bit reglk_ctrl_i[3].", "supports_claim": "The RNG seed is fully exposed whenever debug_mode_i is asserted, regardless of reglk_ctrl_i[3]."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 164, "line_end": 178, "module": "rng_wrapper", "object": "poly128/64/32/16 read mux (addresses 4-11)", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Same debug_mode_i bypass pattern for all polynomial read registers (reglk_ctrl_i[5] ignored).", "supports_claim": "All polynomial configuration data is readable in debug mode regardless of lock."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 186, "line_end": 206, "module": "rng_wrapper", "object": "seed128/64/32/16_big_o, rand_seg_o, cs_state_o read mux (addresses 15-25)", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "All internal LFSR state outputs (current seeds per entropy source, random segments, combiner state) bypass reglk_ctrl_i[7] when debug_mode_i=1.", "supports_claim": "The full internal LFSR state is leaked in debug mode, enabling an attacker to reconstruct past and future random numbers."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/rand_num/rng_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 180, "line_end": 182, "module": "rng_wrapper", "object": "rand_num_big read (addresses 12-13)", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Notably, the final random number output (rand_num_big, addresses 12-13) does NOT use debug_mode_i bypass — it only uses reglk_ctrl_i[3]. This inconsistency means debug mode reveals all intermediate state but not the final output directly, though the intermediate state is sufficient to reconstruct future outputs.", "supports_claim": "Even without direct rand_num exposure in debug mode, the full LFSR state disclosure at addresses 15-25 allows prediction of future random numbers."}], "reasoning_summary": "In rng_wrapper.sv, the read-side always_comb block uses `debug_mode_i ? sensitive_value : (reglk_ctrl_i[N] ? 0 : sensitive_value)` for all seed, polynomial, and internal LFSR state registers. When debug_mode_i=1, all sensitive values are returned without consulting any lock bit. This enables complete disclosure of the RNG internal state (seeds, polynomials, intermediate LFSR values, combiner state) to any entity that can assert debug_mode_i. With full LFSR state, an attacker can reconstruct all past and future random numbers generated by the system.", "security_impact": "Full internal RNG state (seeds for all four LFSR sizes, polynomials, current LFSR register values, combiner state) is leaked to any entity that can assert debug_mode_i. This breaks the unpredictability of the RNG, compromising all cryptographic operations that depend on it (key generation, nonce generation, etc.).", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "Whether debug_mode_i can be asserted post-boot (when firmware has set lock bits) depends on the JTAG authentication strength not fully visible in this scope. The vulnerability is structural regardless of the JTAG strength.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Remove debug_mode_i as a bypass for read access to locked registers. Debug reads should either be gated by an authenticated session or prohibited after lock bits are set.", "Implement a strict policy: once reglk_ctrl_i bits are set, neither debug_mode_i nor any other signal should override them.", "Consider whether any debug read access to raw LFSR state is operationally necessary; if not, remove it entirely."]}], "no_finding_reason": "N/A – findings were identified.", "global_uncertainty": "The JTAG authentication module (dmi_jtag) and the access control table module (acct_wrapper/reglk_wrapper) are referenced but not present in this scope, so the exact conditions under which debug_mode_i and jtag_unlock are asserted cannot be fully confirmed. The PKT and RNG vulnerabilities are structural and visible purely from the RTL source; their exploitability is conditioned on the attacker having access to the AXI interface of the respective peripherals (gated by acc_ctrl_c in riscv_peripherals.sv). Additionally, the fuse_mem module does not implement any read-access control itself — all protection is expected at the PKT wrapper level, reinforcing the severity of the default-assignment bypass in FIND-001."}
